original_abstract: Ecuador acknowledges the need to improve infrastructure and resources for educational inclusion, but it faces challenges in effective implementation compared to developed countries that have made advancements in this area. The objective of this research was to map the regulations and practices related to the implementation of inclusive infrastructure and educational resources at the international level, identifying knowledge gaps and opportunities for adaptation in Ecuador. An exploratory theoretical review was conducted following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, using searches in academic databases and official documents. Qualitative and regulatory studies from the United States, Finland, Canada, and Japan were selected, analyzing 16 scientific articles and 11 official documents. The results reveal that Ecuador faces challenges in the implementation of inclusive regulations, particularly in infrastructure and resources, highlighting the need to establish national accessibility standards, invest in assistive technologies, and offer continuous teacher training to enhance educational inclusion. The research uncovered a negative cycle where the lack of effective implementation of inclusive regulations perpetuates inequality and reinforces institutional inertia. For successful reform, the regulatory structure, resource management, and educational culture in Ecuador must be addressed simultaneously.
humanized_abstract: In Ecuador, as well as in other countries, it is recognised that it is essential to improve the infrastructure and material resources that will enable educational inclusion to be developed. However, the country has a long way to go, a far cry from what has been achieved in developed countries. Thus, the main objective of this investigation was to identify the regulations and practices that are implemented in relation to the infrastructure and materials that promote the inclusion of students with special educational needs at the international level, identifying possible gaps and areas for potential adaptations in Ecuador. An exploratory theoretical review was carried out following PRISMA-ScR guidelines by consulting the scientific literature in various academic databases, as well as official government documents. In this respect, qualitative and regulatory studies were consulted from the United States, Finland, Canada and Japan. In total, 16 scientific articles and 11 official documents were consulted. The results showed that there are a series of obstacles that Ecuador must resolve, especially when it comes to implementing inclusive regulations in relation to infrastructure and materials, such as the need to establish national accessibility criteria, increase access to assistive technologies, as well as the need for permanent teacher training, so that educational inclusion can be effective. The investigations reveal a vicious circle of low educational inclusion and of little effect on the laws and regulations for accessibility of infrastructure and materials, which in turn maintain the existing social inequality and produce great resistance in institutions to apply real changes. Therefore, it is necessary that all the regulations, management of resources and the educational culture of Ecuador are adapted in order for the reforms to have an impact.
